3: Samuel's call to national repentance uses the technical vocabulary of covenant renewal: hāšîḇû ʾet lᵉḇaḇḵem ʾel YHWH wəʿiḇdûhû lᵉḇaḏô — 'Return your hearts to YHWH and serve him alone.' The verbs šûḇ (return/repent) and ʿāḇaḏ (serve/worship) are the covenant's core demands. The call to remove the bəʿālîm and ʿaštārôt — the plural of Baal and Astarte, the dominant Canaanite male and female deities — is the standard formula for covenant renewal (cf. Joshua 24:14; Judges 10:16). Lᵉḇaḏô (him alone) is the exclusivity principle of the Shema: YHWH's covenant requires singular devotion, not mere priority. Jesus cites the Shema's lᵉḇaḏô principle directly: 'Worship the Lord your God and serve him only' (Matthew 4:10, citing Deuteronomy 6:13).
12: Samuel erects the memorial stone and names it ʾeḇen hāʿēzer — 'stone of help': wayyiqrāʾ ʾet šəmāh ʾeḇen hāʿēzer lēʾmōr ʿad hēnnāh ʿăzārānû YHWH — 'he called its name Stone of Help, saying, “Until now YHWH has helped us.”' This is a counter-naming: in 4:1 Israel was defeated at a place that would become Ebenezer (named before the battle), a name that now takes on a new meaning. Samuel plants a covenant-memory marker in the landscape — YHWH's faithfulness made permanent in geography. The ʿad hēnnāh ('until now') phrase is the covenant retrospect formula: all of YHWH's past faithfulness concentrates in this moment. The stone of help is the OT equivalent of the NT's 'He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion' (Philippians 1:6) — divine faithfulness marked and remembered.
1: Vayavo’u anshe Qiryat-ye’arim (“the men of Kiriath-jearim came”). Kiriath-jearim (city of forests) was on the Judah-Benjamin border, identified with modern Abu Ghosh. The ark was placed in the house of Abinadab (beit Avinadav) on the hill (bagiv’ah). Eleazar his son (Et-El’azar beno) was consecrated (qiddeshu oto) to guard (lishmor) the ark. The consecration verb qadash (piel: to set apart for holy purpose) designates Eleazar as the ark’s dedicated guardian. The informal consecration at a private home rather than at the tabernacle reflects the post-Shiloh disruption of the central sanctuary.
2: Vayehi miyor’adi ha-aron beQiryat-ye’arim vayirbu ha-yamim vayihyu esrim shanah (“from the day the ark remained in Kiriath-jearim a long time passed — twenty years”). The phrase vayirbu ha-yamim (the days multiplied/accumulated) expresses the long duration colloquially before the specific figure twenty years is given. Vayinahu khol-beit Yisra’el acharei YHWH (“all the house of Israel mourned and yearned after the LORD”). The verb nachah (niphal: to lament, mourn; or to be drawn toward) is textually debated. Some read it as “lamented” (mourning the ark’s absence); others as “was drawn toward/longed for” YHWH. Either reading captures the spiritual hunger that precedes Samuel’s national revival call in 1 Samuel 7:3.
4: The people obeyed: vayasiru benei Yisra’el et-ha-b’alim ve-et-ha-ashtarot (“the Israelites put away the Baals and the Ashtaroth”). The Baals (ha-b’alim) and Ashtaroth (ha-ashtarot) are collective terms for the Canaanite storm deity and fertility goddess respectively. B’al (master/husband/owner) was the chief storm deity; Astarte/Asherah was his consort and fertility goddess. These are the standard idolatrous alternatives to YHWH throughout Judges and Samuel. Vaya’avdu et YHWH levado (“and served the LORD alone”): levado (alone/only) is the monotheistic dedication term, echoing the Shema’s YHWH echad.
5: Samuel’s call to assemble at Mizpah (qibetzu et-khol Yisra’el ha-Mitzpah). Mizpah was a significant cultic and judicial gathering place in Benjamin (Judges 20:1; 1 Samuel 21:1, 8; later a prominent site in the period of the judges). Samuel’s declaration: ve-etpallel ba’adkem el YHWH (“I will intercede for you before the LORD”). The verb palal (hitpael: intercede/pray on behalf of) identifies Samuel’s intercessory role. Moses was the paradigmatic intercessor (Exodus 32:11-13; Numbers 14:13-19); Samuel stands in the same tradition. 1 Samuel 12:23: “far be it from me to sin against the LORD by ceasing to pray for you.”
6: At Mizpah: they drew water and poured it out before YHWH (vayyishavu mayim vayishpechu lifnei YHWH). The water-pouring rite is unique here — not prescribed in the Mosaic legislation. It appears to be a penitential rite signifying the outpouring of soul/spirit (cf. Hannah’s “pouring out my soul” at 1 Samuel 1:15; Lamentations 2:19). They fasted (vayatzumu) and confessed: chatanu laYHWH (“we have sinned against the LORD”). The three-part penitential sequence — water-pouring, fasting, verbal confession — anticipates the formal confession liturgy of Neh 9 and Dan 9. Vayishpot Shemu’el et-benei Yisra’el ba-Mitzpah: Samuel’s role as judge at Mizpah is given in the same verse as the national assembly — the revival and the judicial function are simultaneous.
7: The Philistines heard about the assembly and marched up (vaya’alu sarnei Pelishtim el Yisra’el). Vayishme’u benei Yisra’el vayire’u mipnei Pelishtim (“the Israelites, hearing this, were afraid of the Philistines”). The juxtaposition is pointed: Israel just assembled for national repentance and immediately faces military threat. The fear (yare’) is genuine — the word is standard military fear vocabulary. The newly repentant community must now discover whether YHWH’s response to repentance extends to military deliverance.
8: The people’s request to Samuel: al-tachrash mimmennu mizzaqeq el YHWH elohenu (“do not stop crying out to the LORD our God for us”). The verb charash (be silent, refrain) negated: “do not be silent.” Zaqaq is unusual — the MT has mizzaqeq el YHWH; some read mize’oq (from crying out). Either way, the request is for unceasing intercession. Veyoshi’enu miyad Pelishtim (“that he may save us from the hand of the Philistines”): the request combines intercession and expected deliverance — the same connection Samuel will make in 1 Samuel 12:23.
9: Samuel offered a nursing lamb (taleh chalav echad) as a whole burnt offering (olah kalil laYHWH). Taleh (nursing lamb) is a tender designation — a young lamb still dependent on its mother. Olah kalil is the wholly-consumed offering; kalil (complete/total) emphasizes that nothing is retained by the offerer. Vayizaq Shemu’el el YHWH ba’ad Yisra’el (“Samuel cried out to the LORD on Israel’s behalf”). The double action — offering and intercession simultaneously — defines the prophetic-priestly function Samuel exercises: the offering is the altar-expression; the cry is the verbal expression of the same petition. Vaya’anehu YHWH: “the LORD answered him” — the terse divine response confirms the intercession’s efficacy.
10: As Samuel was offering the olah, the Philistines advanced to attack. YHWH ra’am (thundered) with qol gadol (a great voice/thunder) against the Philistines and vayyahamem (threw them into panic/confusion). Ra’am (thunder) is the theophanic weapon of YHWH: Psalms 18:13-14; 1 Samuel 29:3-9 describe YHWH’s voice as thunder that splits oaks and shakes the wilderness. Hamam (confuse/panic) is the divine-warrior verb from the Exodus and conquest: Exodus 14:24 (“YHWH looked down and threw the Egyptians into confusion”); Joshua 10:10 (at Gibeon). The combination of thunder-theophany and hamam-panic is a signature formula for YHWH fighting on Israel’s behalf.
11: Israel pursued the Philistines from Mizpah and struck them down as far as Beth-car (ad-tachat bet-kar). Beth-car is unidentified; the name may mean “house of the lamb/young ram.” The pursuit pattern (enemy routed, Israel pursues) is standard conquest/battle narrative: Joshua 10:10; Judges 4:16. The geographical terminus marks the completeness of the victory.
13: Vayikannu ha-Pelishtim velo yasafu od lavo bigevul Yisra’el (“the Philistines were brought low and did not again enter Israelite territory”). Kana’ (niphal: be subdued/humbled) is the term for YHWH’s humbling of Israel’s enemies after divine-warrior victories (Judges 8:28; 1 Samuel 11:33). The clause vatehi yad YHWH ba-Pelishtim kol yemei Shemu’el (“the hand of the LORD was against the Philistines throughout Samuel’s lifetime”) is a retrospective summary that frames Samuel’s entire judgeship as characterized by this divine restraint on Philistia — a marked contrast with the chaos of the Ark Narrative period.
14: The territorial restoration: cities “from Ekron to Gath” (meEqron ve-ad Gath) were returned to Israel. Ve-et gevulotehen hitzil Yisra’el miyad Pelishtim (“Israel recovered their surrounding territories from Philistine control”). Natsal (hiphil: rescue/deliver) is the standard deliverance verb of the Judges-Samuel era (cf. 1 Samuel 4:3; 1 Samuel 7:8). Vayehi shalom bein Yisra’el ubein ha-Emori (“there was also peace between Israel and the Amorites”). The Amorites (a blanket term for pre-Israelite Canaanite peoples) are mentioned here as a separate peace agreement, suggesting the Philistine defeat also stabilized relations with the inland populations.
15: Vayishpot Shemu’el et Yisra’el kol yemei chayyav (“Samuel judged Israel throughout his lifetime”). The lifelong judicial tenure contrasts with the sequential judges of the book of Judges who arose, delivered, and died. Samuel’s continuous tenure makes him transitional: he is the last of the judges and the inaugurator of the monarchic period. The shafat (judge) function in the Deuteronomistic framework includes both military deliverance and juridical administration; Samuel performs both.
16: Samuel’s annual circuit: halachu mide-shanah beshanah vesavar Beit-el ve-ha-Gilgal ha-Mitzpah (“each year he made a circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah”). The three sites are major cultic-judicial centers: Bethel (patriarchal altar, Genesis 12:8; 1 Samuel 28:19), Gilgal (first Israelite camp in Canaan, Joshua 4:19-20), Mizpah (assembly site, 1 Samuel 7:5-11). Samuel’s circuit institutionalizes itinerant justice, making the judicial system accessible rather than centralized. The circuit judge as travelling administrator of justice anticipates the later administrative structures of Jehoshaphat (2 Chronicles 19:5-11).
17: Samuel’s home base was Ramah (uve-Ramah ki-sham beito), where he lived, judged Israel (shafat et Yisra’el), and built an altar to YHWH (vayiven sham mizbeach laYHWH). The altar at Ramah is the only altar Samuel builds; its construction at his home suggests a semi-permanent local sanctuary. Pre-Solomonic altars at local sites were not prohibited; the centralization legislation of Deut 12 was not yet fully operative (or was routinely set aside in this period). The altar functions as the visible marker of Samuel’s priestly-prophetic office at Ramah.
